coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on dual-responsive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for enzyme-free and visual quantitative detection of Cu2+ and glyphosate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Xinyu
Liu, Ziyi
Wang, Linfan
Gao, Liwei
Wang, Dingfeng
Zhang, Ting
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and selective detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and glyphosate (GLY) is crucial to environmental pollution control, especially when using a simple and portable device. In this study, an enzyme-free and visual fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is successfully designed, featuring strong blue fluorescence and excellent stability. The sensor shows a linear response to Cu2+ over a wide detection range of 0.048 to 40 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD, 48 nM), resulting in fluorescence quenching at 435 nm. Meanwhile, the rapid enhancement in fluorescence intensity of the quenched N-CDs/Cu2+ system can be triggered by GLY owing to the strong complexation between Cu2+ and GLY, with a LOD of GLY as low as 0.24 μM. Furthermore, we develop a smartphone-assisted sensing platform using N-CDs/polyvinyl alcohol films, which captures and analyzes fluorescence images to detect Cu2+ and GLY with sensitive LODs of 73 nM and 0.32 μM, respectively. Satisfactory recoveries, ranging from 94.0 % to 104.6 %, are achieved in real sample detection, implying the potential application in the field of enzyme-free and on-site pesticides detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Enzyme-free
Cu2+ and glyphosate
Color recognizer software
Smartphone-assisted detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection for Cu2+ and GLY using N-CDs
Detection of Cu2+ and GLY using smartphone-assisted sensing platform
Analysis of Cu2+ and GLY in real samples
Detection strategy of Cu2+ using N-CDs
Detection strategy of GLY using N-CDs/Cu2+ system
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on fluorescence sensor
Application for detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs. Inset depicts the HR-TEM of N-CDs. (b) Size distribution of N-CDs. (c) AFM image of N-CDs. Inset shows the height distribution of N-CDs. (d) XRD pattern and (e) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs. XPS spectra of full scan (f), C 1s (g), N 1s (h) and O 1s (i) of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. Insets depict the aqueous solution of N-CDs under sunlight and ultraviolet light. (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (330–420 nm). (c) Fluorescent matric scanning map of N-CDs. The stabilities of N-CDs under different conditions: exposed to sunlight over varying storage time(d), in NaCl aqueous solutions at different concentrations (e) and across a range of pH values (f).

(a) The PL spectra of N-CDs with increasing concentrations of Cu2+. (b) Plot of F2 /F1 and Cu2+ concentrations (0–1000 µM). (c) The linear range of F2 /F1 at various Cu2+ concentrations (0.048–40 µM). UV–vis absorption spectra (d), FT-IR spectra (e) and PL decay profiles (f) of N-CDs and N-CDs after quenching.

(a) PL spectra of N-CDs (i); N-CDs + GLY (ii); N-CDs/Cu2+ (iii); N-CDs/Cu2++GLY (iv). (b) PL spectra of N-CDs/Cu2+ with increasing concentrations of GLY. (c) Plot of F4 /F3 and GLY concentration (0–100 µM). Inset shows the linearity between F4 /F3 and GLY concentrations (0.24–20 µM). (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/Cu2+ and N-CDs/Cu2++GLY. FT-IR spectra (e) and PL decay profiles (f) of N-CDs, N-CDs/Cu2+ and N-CDs/Cu2++GLY. (g) The illustration of GLY detection mechanism.
The visual quantitation detection of Cu2+ and GLY with the use of smartphone-assisted sensing platform.
Synthesis and application of the N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on dual-responsive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for enzyme-free and visual quantitative detection of Cu2+ and glyphosate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo 315800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo 315800, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and selective detection of copper ion (Cu2+) and glyphosate (GLY) is crucial to environmental pollution control, especially when using a simple and portable device. In this study, an enzyme-free and visual fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) is successfully designed, featuring strong blue fluorescence and excellent stability. The sensor shows a linear response to Cu2+ over a wide detection range of 0.048 to 40 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD, 48 nM), resulting in fluorescence quenching at 435 nm. Meanwhile, the rapid enhancement in fluorescence intensity of the quenched N-CDs/Cu2+ system can be triggered by GLY owing to the strong complexation between Cu2+ and GLY, with a LOD of GLY as low as 0.24 μM. Furthermore, we develop a smartphone-assisted sensing platform using N-CDs/polyvinyl alcohol films, which captures and analyzes fluorescence images to detect Cu2+ and GLY with sensitive LODs of 73 nM and 0.32 μM, respectively. Satisfactory recoveries, ranging from 94.0 % to 104.6 %, are achieved in real sample detection, implying the potential application in the field of enzyme-free and on-site pesticides detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread utilization of heavy metal ions in various industrial processes, such as machinery manufacturing, electronic component processing and dyeing, has become increasingly prevalent because of ongoing advancements in global industrialization [1]. Among the heavy metal ions discharged from industrial wastewater, copper ion (Cu2+) is particularly notable as a toxic and harmful element that can bio-accumulate through the food chain. Although trace amounts of Cu2+ are necessary for several physiological functions in the human body, excessive levels can cause diseases like cirrhosis, diarrhea and vomiting [2]. Conversely, a deficiency in Cu2+ can result in diseases such as osteoporosis, anemia and vitiligo [3]. Therefore, the development of a rapid and precise device for detecting Cu2+ in food samples and environment is both necessary and essential.

Glyphosate (GLY) is the most extensively used herbicide all over the world due to high efficiency and broad-spectrum. However, its continuous application in agriculture has led to its persistent presence in soil, posing significant threats to both human health and the ecological environment. Several studies have reported that GLY can cause respiratory, myocardial infarction and neuromuscular dysfunction. Moreover, GLY is considered as a potential endocrine disruptor, negatively affect cell apoptosis and autophagy [4,5]. Recently, the International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified GLY as a group 2A carcinogen [6]. Given these concerns, it is highly demanded to develop analytical methods for the rapid and accurate detection of GLY.

In recent years, a variety of methods have been devised for the detection of GLY, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, Raman scattering, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, capillary electrophoresis, ion chromatography, and electrochemical methods [7–13]. While these methods offer accuracy and sensitivity, they are often associated with drawbacks such as high cost, complex sample preparation, lack of portability and the need for professional expertise, which impede their wide application. In order to circumvent the reliance on expensive equipment and complex procedures, nanomaterial-assisted fluorescence sensors have garnered considerable attention because of their merits of low cost, high specificity, good sensitivity and rapid response [14]. Among the fluorescence material, carbon dots (CDs), a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been gained growing interest because of their unique advantages, like cost-effective preparation, excellent water solubility, low toxicity and ease of surface functionalization [15]. For example, Zhao et al. utilized nitrogen-doped CDs to detect Cu2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.19 μM by exploiting the fluorescence quenching caused by electron pair sharing between N atoms and Cu2+ [16]. Zhao et al. employed CDs as a fluorescence sensor for the quantitative detection of GLY with LODs of 6.2 μM through an off–on mode [17]. However, the above-mentioned fluorescence sensors still have shortcomings, such as complex preparation and low sensitivity. More importantly, there are limited reports concerning the simultaneous measurement of Cu2+ and GLY [18]. Therefore, there is still a need to develop new fluorescence detection systems that can simultaneously and specifically detect Cu2+ and GLY in a simple, sensitive and specific manner. Besides, to meet the increasing demand for highly sensitive and accurate on-site detection methods, the integration of smartphones into sensing devices has attracted increasing attention [19]. This approach offers a low-cost and easy-to-fabricate alternative to complex equipment. Meanwhile, it is well known that naked-eye assessments can be prone to subjective error. The use of smartphones provides an innovative solution to minimize human variability, thereby improving the precision of visual analysis. In view of this, portable devices integrated with smartphones offers promising potential for advancing rapid detection techniques for sensing Cu2+ and GLY.

In this study, water-soluble blue fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are synthesized by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment, utilizing tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) and benzamide. The N-CDs emit strong blue emission (435 nm) with excellent stability at an excitation wavelength of 358 nm. After introducing Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually diminishes due to chelation between Cu2+ and N-CDs based on the combination of static and dynamic quenching effect. Subsequently, with the addition of GLY, GLY can quickly remove Cu2+ from N-CDs and form a strong chelation with Cu2+, leading to the restoration of fluorescence intensity. Consequently, the N-CDs show excellent fluorescence sensing capabilities for the specific and label-free identification of Cu2+ and GLY, realizing the limit of detections (LODs) as low as 48 nM and 0.24 μM, respectively. Moreover, an enzyme-free sensing platform with N-CDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) fluorescence films and smartphone is developed to analyze the concentrations of Cu2+ and GLY in real samples through color recognizer software (Scheme 1 ), which achieves sensitive LODs of 73 nM and 0.32 μM for Cu2+ and GLY, respectively. Consequently, this innovative sensor offers a dependable approach for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ and GLY, while also having potential application in the field of analytical science research.

Tris, benzamide, PVA, CuCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, PbCl2, GeCl2, BaCl2, CoCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, NiCl, ZnCl2, CdCl2, HgCl2, AgCl, KI, NaNO3, Na2CO3, carbofuran, alachlor, malathion, metalaxyl, fenitrothion, parathion, GLY and other chemical materials were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were utilized as received without additional purification processes. Deionized water, produced by a Millipore water purification system, was used throughout the experimental procedures.

The morphology and crystalline structure of N-CDs were observed by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, D5005, SIEMENS, Germany). Atomic force microscope (AFM) was utilized to measure the height of the N-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a K-Alpha Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Nicolet-6700 spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of N-CDs were collected at room temperature on a UV-2007 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer. PL decay profiles were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 instrument (Hitachi, Japan). Raman spectrum was acquired under an excitation wavelength of 358 nm on a HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer.

N-CDs was synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal methodology. First, Tris (2 mmol) and benzamide (2 mmol) were thoroughly mixed in 10 mL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 100 mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for a duration of 15 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Finally, the filtered solution was refrigerated at 4 °C for use in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for Cu2+ and GLY using N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, varying concentrations of Cu2+ were added to N-CDs solution at room temperature. After reacting for 1 min, the fluorescence excitation-emission matrices of the samples were measured. For GLY detection, 200 μM of Cu2+ was mixed with 3 mL of N-CDs solution and agitated until the fluorescence intensity of mixture was fully quenched. Subsequently, 1 mL of GLY solutions at varying concentrations were introduced to the mixture and incubated for 1 min. Fluorescence spectra were then obtained to monitor changes in fluorescence intensity. To evaluate anti-interference properties, both Cu2+ and GLY were tested by introducing other commonly used stock solutions (including metal ions, amino acids and pesticides) in a similar way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and GLY using smartphone-assisted sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve rapid response and simplify the visual identification of Cu2+ and GLY, a smartphone-assisted device based on N-CDs films was developed. Firstly, N-CDs-based films were prepared by combining N-CDs or N-CDs/Cu2+ with non-luminescent PVA. After evaporating the mixture onto a polytetrafluoroethylene plate, the obtained films were shaped into circular discs and integrated into castor oil-based waterborne polyurethane (CWPU) films modified by amino-propyltriethoxysilane (KH550). These films were applied to practical samples such as apples, oranges and cucumbers, which had been sprayed with Cu2+ or GLY respectively. The 200 μL of sample solution was then transferred onto the N-CDs/PVA films using a pipette. Subsequently, the circle-like films were placed into a 3D-printed detection apparatus, assisted by HUAWEI P50 Pro smartphone camera (rear-facing camera stats: 50.0 megapixels, 40.0 megapixels, 13.0 megapixels; front-facing camera stats: 13.0 megapixels) to capture fluorescence images under 365 nm ultraviolet light. These images were analyzed using color identification software installed onto the smartphone. This software assessed the color and converted it into corresponding red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values. Finally, the sum of RGB values was calculated by the subsequent formula:
(1)
Sum RGB value s = R + G + B

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cu2+ and GLY in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh apples, oranges and cucumbers used for food analysis were procured from a market in Ningbo, China. The tap water collected from Ningbo University was utilized for further studies. Then, apples, oranges and cucumbers samples were thoroughly minced before adding 50 mL of water and subjecting them to ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 5 min was employed to obtain the supernatant. To detect Cu2+ in actual samples, different concentrations of Cu2+ solutions were mixed with the processed samples. For GLY determination, varying concentrations of GLY solutions were added to the processed samples after the introduction of Cu2+. Finally, these mixed solutions underwent ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, resulting in super-hydrophobic fluorescence films for further analysis using a smartphone-integrated sensing platform.

Characterization methods are employed to confirm the successful synthesis of N-CDs. TEM image in Fig. 1 (a) reveals a uniform distribution of N-CDs with a nearly spherical shape. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image, shown in the inset, demonstrates a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which can be ascribed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [20]. The size distribution of N-CDs ranges from 2.5 to 6.5 nm with an average particle size at 4.5 nm (Fig. 1(b)). Additionally, as shown in Fig. 1(c), the topographic height of N-CDs, based on AFM measurements, primarily ranges from 0.8 to 3.1 nm, indicating a few layers of graphite in the carbon core. XRD pattern of N-CDs displays an apparent peak at approximately 23°, similar to the lattice spacing of graphite, suggesting an amorphous nature for the N-CDs (Fig. 1(d)) [21]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S1) shows two characteristic peaks at 1349.68 cm−1 (the disorder D band) and 1580.14 cm−1 (the graphitic G band), with a G/D band ratio of 1.12, confirming the high degree of graphitization of the N-CDs [22].

The chemical constituents and elemental distribution of N-CDs are analyzed using FT-IR and XPS. Fig. 1(e) reveals that the characteristic peaks correspond to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H, ranging from 3130 to 3615 cm−1. Additionally, the CC and CO exhibit stretching vibrational absorption bands at 1409 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, respectively, while the absorption peak at 1071 cm−1 is derived from CN tensile vibration [23]. As shown in Fig. 1(f), three main peaks are detected in the XPS full spectrum of the N-CDs at 286.08 eV, 400.08 eV and 532.08 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1(g)) is resolved into four peaks at 284.88 eV, 285.18 eV, 286.68 eV and 287.68 eV, which are ascribed to the CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively [24]. The N 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 1(h) confirms the successful incorporation of nitrogen into silicon quantum dots, demonstrating peaks at 399.58 eV, 400.58 eV and 401.08 eV, which correspond to NH, pyrrolic N and CNH, respectively [25]. As presented in Fig. 1(i), peaks at 531.78 eV, 532.08 eV and 533.68 eV suggest the presence of OC, OC and ON, respectively [26]. These findings demonstrate that N-CDs are successfully synthesized with a rich presence of hydroxyl and amino groups, facilitating a uniform dispersion in water.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the optical properties of N-CDs. The N-CDs exhibit two characteristic absorption regions: an absorption band at 263 nm corresponding to the π-π* electron transition of the CC bond [27], and a weaker absorption band ranging from 288 to 320 nm, attributed to surface states or the N-related defect bonds [28]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CDs are 358 nm and 435 nm, respectively. Accordingly, the inset of Fig. 2(a) shows the N-CDs solution under natural sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light. Additionally, the emission wavelength of N-CDs exhibits a red shift when excited at wavelengths ranging from 330 to 420 nm, indicating their excitation-dependent behavior (Fig. 2(b)), which results from different emissive states [29]. The excitation-emission map further reveals a large fluorescence region for N-CDs with λex = 358 nm and λem = 435 nm (Fig. 2(c)).

The stabilities of N-CDs are investigated under various conditions to evaluate their suitability for further applications. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs shows minimal attenuation even after 24 days of sunlight irradiation (Fig. 2(d)). Meanwhile, the fluorescence behavior of N-CDs remains unaffected by changes in ion concentration (Fig. 2(e)), with NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 2 M. Fig. 2(f) illustrates the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at different pH levels. Initially, the fluorescence intensity increases, peaking at pH = 6. However, it subsequently decreases as the pH rises from 7 to 14 because of deprotonation under alkaline conditions [30]. These results highlight the excellent stability of N-CDs under various environmental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection strategy of Cu2+ using N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After successfully synthesizing and characterizing N-CDs, the sensing capabilities towards commonly cations and anions are examined using fluorescence spectra. In the experiments, 200 µM solutions containing various substance including Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ge2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ni+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, I−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, are subjected to treatment with N-CDs solution and variations in fluorescence intensity are observed. The detected results reveal that the Cu2+ and Fe3+exhibit a quenched in fluorescence, while other ions have little change. It is noteworthy that Fe3+ does not exert the same inhibitory effect as Cu2+, thereby indicating the probe is selective for Cu2+ detection (Figs. S2–S3). Fig. 3 (a) shows the PL intensities decrease with increasing concentrations of Cu2+. The plot of F2 /F1 versus the concentration of Cu2+ from 0 to 1000 µM (F2 and F1 represent the PL intensity of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively) is conducted, with a fitting curve analysis in the range of 0.048 to 40 µM (Fig. 3(b)). Using the formula of 3σ/k, the LOD of Cu2+ is calculated to be 48 nM, where σ and k represent the signal standard deviation and the slope, respectively (Fig. 3(c)). Moreover, a comparison of the detection method for Cu2+ in this study with those reported in other researches demonstrates that this fluorescence sensor possesses strong analytical capabilities for detecting Cu2+ (Table S1). Additionally, Fig. S4 presents the reaction time for sensing Cu2+, showing that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs exceeds 90 % within 100 s of interaction with Cu2+ and subsequently remains relatively stable. This rapid response further underscores the potential of N-CDs as an efficient and rapid fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+.

To investigate the detection mechanism of N-CDs on Cu2+, a series of systematic experiments are conducted. Fig. S5 reveals that the UV–vis absorption peak of Cu2+ is positioned around 211 nm, with no overlap with the PLE and PL spectra of N-CDs (358 nm excitation and 435 nm emission), indicating that the inner filter effect (IFE) and förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) are unlikely to occur [31]. Additionally, a strong absorption peak in the range of 240–263 nm is observed in the N-CDs solution, which blue-shifts to 236–257 nm when Cu2+ is added (Fig. 3(d)), signifying that complexations are generated during the quenching process due to the static quenching effect (SQE) [32]. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3(e)), the O-H/N-H peak (from 3130 to 3615 cm−1) is notably reduced after the addition of Cu2+, and the peak at 1071 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN, disappears. These results indicate that Cu2+ interacts with OH and NH groups of N-CDs. Furthermore, Fig. 3(f) implies that the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs + Cu2+ are 4.71 ns and 6.12 ns, respectively. The varying PL decay profiles indicate that Cu2+ also quenches the fluorescence of N-CDs through the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) [33]. According to the above results, we conclude that Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism involves both static and dynamic quenching processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection strategy of GLY using N-CDs/Cu2+ system
ce:section.ce:para: Previous research has demonstrated that the abundant amine, carboxylate and phosphate groups in GLY exhibit a remarkably strong chelation interaction with Cu2+, forming a Jahn-Teller distorted octahedron. In addition, the tridentate combination of GLY, including carboxylate oxygen, the nitrogen atom of the monoprotonated secondary amine and phosphonate oxygen atoms, chelates with Cu2+ to form two adjacent chelate rings positioned in the equatorial plane [34,35]. As a result, it provides an excellent opportunity to establish a fluorescence sensor for detecting GLY by combining N-CDs with Cu2+.

As depicted in Fig. 4 (a), the N-CDs display a strong emission peak at 435 nm when excited at a wavelength of 358 nm (i). There is no apparent change in the PL intensity of N-CDs after the introduction of GLY (ii). Nevertheless, the presence of Cu2+ leads to a significant quenching of the PL intensity of N-CDs (iii), which is restored upon the introduction of GLY (iv). Thus, a nonenzymatic fluorescence sensor is established based on the turn-on strategy of N-CDs/Cu2+ system for detecting GLY. As performed in Fig. 4(b), with increasing GLY concentrations from 0 to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity gradually recovers to nearly 90 % within two minutes (Fig. S5). By calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio of F4 /F3 against the concentrations of GLY (F4 and F3 represent the PL intensity of detection system before and after adding GLY, respectively), a strong linear correlation is observed for GLY concentrations ranging from 0.24 to 20 μM, and the LOD of GLY is 0.24 μM (Fig. 4(c)). Compared with other reported GLY detection methods, our sensing method is highly practical and suitable for monitoring GLY (Table S2). To demonstrate that it can be used as a sensitive and highly selective fluorescence sensor, we conduct various substance test including Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ge2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Ni+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, I−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, urea, vitamin C, carbofuran, alachlor, malathion, metalaxyl, fenitrothion and parathion. The fluorescence intensity of the system remains almost unchanged when these substances are present alongside N-CDs/Cu2++GLY. This observation confirms that the presence of these substance does not interfere with the detection process. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs/Cu2+ demonstrates exceptional anti-interference capability for detecting GLY, even in the presence of other substances (Fig. S6).

To clarify the recovery mechanism, we conduct various analyses, including UV–vis absorption spectra, zeta potential measurements, PL decay profiles and XPS spectrum, to explore the interaction mechanism between N-CDs/Cu2+ and GLY. Fig. 4(d) shows the absorption increases dramatically with the addition of GLY. Meanwhile, Fig. 4(e) performs that the zeta potential of N-CDs/Cu2+ decreases from a positive value to a negative value after the addition of GLY, confirming the formation of a GLY-Cu2+ complex, which aligns with the UV–vis absorption spectra. Upon the addition of GLY, the PL decay profile of N-CDs/Cu2++GLY does not show significant changes compared to the initial PL decay profile of N-CDs (Fig. 4(f)). This can be attributed to the stronger interaction between GLY and Cu2+ compared to that between N-CDs and Cu2+, resulting in the complete stripping of Cu2+ from the N-CDs/Cu2+ system [36]. Moreover, Fig. S7 presents the Cu 2p narrow scan XPS spectra for N-CDs/Cu2+ in the absence and presence of GLY. The peak assigned to Cu 2p1/2 at 952.1 eV shifts to 952.7 eV after the addition of GLY, indicating a stronger bonding capacity between GLY and Cu2+ than with N-CDs [37]. Overall, the recovery mechanism is attributable to the superior binding capacity of GLY with Cu2+, which allows it to competitively trap Cu2+ from the surface of N-CDs. The detection mechanism for GLY is schematically depicted in Fig. 4(g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the visual detection of Cu2+ and GLY, we have developed a real-time smartphone-assisted optical sensing platform, which consists of N-CDs/polyvinyl alcohol super-hydrophobic films, a portable black box and a smartphone preinstalled with color recognition software. Firstly, we combine N-CDs or N-CDs/Cu2+ solution with PVA to create a fluorescent visual sensor. Next, the N-CDs/PVA or N-CDs/Cu2+/PVA composite is integrated with modified CWPU to produce an enhanced composite film. The film consists of two areas: the hydrophobic area (the modified CWPU film) and the detection area (N-CDs/PVA or N-CDs/Cu2+/PVA film) (Fig. S8). Notably, the hydrophobic area exhibits chemical resistance, water resistance, exceptional thermal stability and storage stability [38]. This hydrophobic circle-like film allows the test liquid to be directed towards the center from all sides, facilitating easier use of the analysis device in practical applications. In addition, the black detection box is equipped with a mini ultraviolet light emitter (100 mW) that operates at a wavelength of 365 nm, a viewing aperture, switch, optical filter and sample cell (Fig. S9). To handle data, a color recognition software called Color Toolkit is used on the smartphone to capture and analyze the fluorescence images. As a result, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform can be applied for on-site detection of Cu2+ and GLY.

During the detection process, different concentrations of Cu2+ solutions are dripped onto N-CDs/PVA films. After a 2-minutes reaction, these films, each containing different concentrations of absorbed Cu2+, are placed in a sample cell inside a black box. A smartphone camera captures fluorescence images, which are then converted into corresponding RGB values using color recognition software. Subsequently, various concentrations of GLY solutions are dripped onto the N-CDs/Cu2+/PVA films and measured in a similar way. Fig. 5 displays a strong linear relationship between the sum of RGB values and the concentrations of both Cu2+ and GLY, and the LODs are calculated to be 73 nM (linear range 0.073–40 μM) and 0.32 μM (linear range 0.32–20 μM), respectively. This approach realizes the visual and quantitative detection of Cu2+ and GLY without the need for bulky laboratory instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-assisted sensing platform with N-CDs/PVA films successfully detects Cu2+ and GLY in real samples, such as apples, oranges and cucumbers, using the standard method. Video S1 shows the detection process for real samples. By applying different concentrations of Cu2+ and GLY onto the films and utilizing a color identification software to acquire RGB values and analyte contents, the recoveries in three samples range from 94.0 % to 102.2 %, 97.0 % to 104.6 % and 95.8 % to 102.2 % (Table S3), respectively. These results demonstrate high accuracy, with a low relative standard deviation (%RSD) of less than 2.41, indicating excellent reliability for practical applications.

In conclusion, the fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs, using Tris and benzamide as precursors, is developed, exhibiting strong blue emission and enabling sensitive detection for Cu2+ and GLY. In this enzyme-free sensor, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is effectively quenched by Cu2+ due to the static-dynamic quenching effect. Upon introducing GLY into the system, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/Cu2+ progressively increases, reaching nearly 90 % of its initial state, due to the stronger complexation of Cu2+ with GLY compared to N-CDs. The detection limits for Cu2+ and GLY can be reached as low as 48 nM and 0.24 μM, respectively. Moreover, we propose composite films consisting of N-CDs/PVA, with the hydrophobic area of modified CWPU film and the detection area of N-CDs/PVA film. These composite films are successfully applied in a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for the rapid detection of Cu2+ and GLY. Satisfactory recoveries, ranging from 94.0 % to 104.6 %, are achieved in real samples analyses. Therefore, this sensing platform provides promising potential for enzyme-free quantitation of Cu2+ and GLY and offers an innovative way for developing an on-site pesticides analysis platform.

Xinyu Luo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ziyi Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Linfan Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis. Liwei Gao: Supervision. Dingfeng Wang: Supervision. Ting Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Da Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from Talent-introduction Training Special Project of NBPT (NZ23RC03), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61604084) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data [Figures (Fig. S1–S10). Video (Video S1)] to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112166.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
Figs. S1–S10.
